concept_name: Umbrella Tilt Angle — How Far Forward to Tilt When Running
source_book: DC Pandey 6.12 — umbrella tilt problem; split from rain_umbrella

real_world_anchor.primary: A Mumbai local commuter running from Dadar station exit to a rickshaw stand during a heavy shower. Rain falls vertically at 5 m/s; she runs at 4 m/s. She holds her umbrella tilted forward at arctan(4/5) = 38.7 degrees — exactly perpendicular to the apparent rain direction. Hold it straight up and rain wets her front. Tilt too far and rain wets her back. Right tilt = dry commute. This one equation saves a lakh uniforms every monsoon.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A delivery boy on a Hero Splendor in Bengaluru during a July shower. Rain at 6 m/s, bike at 18 m/s. Umbrella rigged to the helmet tilts at arctan(18/6) = 71.6 degrees — almost horizontal — because at bike speed the rain seems to blast in straight from the front.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A Mars exploration rover driving under suspended dust particles during a dust storm. Particles fall at 0.5 m/s; rover moves at 0.05 m/s. Tilt is arctan(0.1) ≈ 5.7 degrees. Same physics, different planet.

formulas.umbrella_rule: Tilt the umbrella FORWARD from vertical by angle theta such that tan(theta) = v_person / v_rain
formulas.equivalence_with_apparent_velocity: theta = angle that apparent rain velocity makes with the vertical (in the forward direction)
formulas.perpendicular_shield: The umbrella's canopy must be perpendicular to the apparent rain direction to block maximum water.

constraints: Rain assumed to fall vertically in the ground frame (no crosswind).
Tilt angle is measured forward from vertical, in the plane of motion.
Formula is tan (not sin or cos) because the two velocities are perpendicular.

scene_composition.text:
Dadar station, 6:30 PM monsoon rush.

  Rain falls vertically:  v_rain   = 5 m/s (down)
  Commuter sprints east:  v_person = 4 m/s

  Question: at what FORWARD TILT should she hold the umbrella?

  Wrong answer (straight up):
     rain slants in from the front, soaks her jeans.
  Wrong answer (tilted backward):
     rain still hits her from the front, even more exposed.
  Correct answer (tilted forward):
     umbrella shields the forward-approaching apparent rain.

     theta  =  arctan(v_person / v_rain)
            =  arctan(4 / 5)
            =  arctan(0.8)
            ~=  38.7 deg forward
Dadar station commuter in a downpour

tts_sentences.text_en: Dadar station, monsoon rush hour. Rain falls vertically at 5 metres per second; the commuter sprints east at 4.
She holds an umbrella. At what angle forward should she tilt it?
Straight up — rain slants into her jeans. Backward — rain still hits her from the front and she is fully exposed.
Correct: tilt it forward by theta equal to arctan of v person over v rain — that is arctan 4 over 5, about 38.7 degrees.

STATE_2.title: Why tan, Not sin — The Right-Angle Geometry

scene_composition.text:
Right-angle vector triangle:

              apparent rain velocity
                    ||
                    ||  <- along this line, umbrella perpendicular
                    ||
                    ||
         v_rain     ||
        (vertical)  ||
            | __________
            |/|
           -+--+  v_person (horizontal)
            |__|
               |
               |
     theta = angle between apparent rain and vertical

   Right triangle has:
      opposite side = v_person (horizontal)
      adjacent side = v_rain   (vertical)

   tan(theta)  =  opposite / adjacent  =  v_person / v_rain

   Hence the FAMOUS umbrella formula:
       theta = arctan(v_person / v_rain)

tts_sentences.text_en: Why tangent, and not sine or cosine?
Because the person's horizontal velocity and the rain's vertical velocity are PERPENDICULAR. The triangle they form has a 90-degree angle between them.
Tangent equals opposite over adjacent. Opposite is v person — horizontal. Adjacent is v rain — vertical.
So tan theta equals v person divided by v rain. That is the umbrella formula for life.

scene_composition.text:
Umbrella tilt for v_rain = 5 m/s:

   v_person (m/s)  | tilt theta (deg)
   ----------------+-----------------
        0         |    0    (straight up)
        1         |   11.3
        2         |   21.8
        3         |   31.0
        4         |   38.7  <- Dadar commuter
        5         |   45.0  (umbrella at 45 deg)
        8         |   58.0
       15         |   71.6
       25         |   78.7

   Rule of thumb:
      SAME speed as rain -> umbrella at 45 deg.
      DOUBLE the rain speed -> theta ~ 63 deg.
      TEN TIMES the rain -> theta almost horizontal.

tts_sentences.text_en: A quick speed-to-tilt reference, fixing v rain at 5 metres per second.
Walking speed — 21 degrees. Jogging — 31. The Dadar commuter at 4 — 38.7.
When your own speed equals the rain speed, the umbrella is at exactly 45 degrees.
Double your speed to ten times rain speed — and the umbrella lies almost horizontal. Useful for JEE and useful for daily life.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student plugs into sine or cosine by reflex, instead of using tangent.
confusion_patterns: sin or tan for rain tilt

scene_composition.text:
Student's guess:
  "All tilt problems use sine or cosine.
   Let me try  sin(theta) = v_person / v_rain
   or similar."

  On the Dadar example:
   sin(theta) = 4 / 5 = 0.8 -> theta = 53.1 deg

   That's not what the right-angle geometry gives.
   Correct answer (via tan) = 38.7 deg.

   14-degree error.  In board exams, that's a lost mark.

tts_sentences.text_en: A student reaches for sine as a reflex. Sine of theta equals v person over v rain — 0.8 — so theta equals 53 degrees?
No. The correct answer is 38.7 degrees, from tangent.
A 14-degree error is one lost mark in a board exam. Worth getting right.

STATE_2.title: Why tan: Opposite and Adjacent are Both Known

scene_composition.text:
Which trig function to use depends on which sides you KNOW:

   sin(theta)  = opposite / hypotenuse
   cos(theta)  = adjacent / hypotenuse
   tan(theta)  = opposite / adjacent

   In the umbrella problem:
      opposite  =  v_person  (known, 4 m/s)
      adjacent  =  v_rain    (known, 5 m/s)
      hypotenuse = sqrt(4^2 + 5^2) = sqrt(41)  (must be COMPUTED)

   Since we know BOTH legs but NOT the hypotenuse,
   tan is the natural choice.

   tan(theta) = v_person / v_rain  <- clean, one step.

tts_sentences.text_en: The right trig function depends on which sides of the triangle you know.
We know the two legs — v person and v rain — but not the hypotenuse.
Sine needs the hypotenuse. Cosine needs the hypotenuse. Only tangent works directly with just the two legs.

scene_composition.text:
Always sanity-check with limits:

   v_person = 0:
     tan(theta) = 0 / v_rain = 0  -> theta = 0   (hold straight up)  OK
     sin(theta) = 0 / v_rain = 0  -> theta = 0   (looks right, but only because v = 0)

   v_person = v_rain:
     tan(theta) = 1             -> theta = 45    (matches intuition)
     sin(theta) = v_p / v_r = 1 -> theta = 90    (wrong -- would mean horizontal)

   v_person  -> infinity:
     tan(theta) -> infinity     -> theta -> 90   (matches: rain horizontal)
     sin(theta) -> impossible   (ratio > 1)

   Tan passes every limit.  Sin fails.
   Tan is the right function.

tts_sentences.text_en: One quick limit check to cement it.
At v person equal to v rain, tangent gives 45 degrees — matches intuition. Sine would give 90 — which makes no sense.
As v person goes to infinity, tangent goes to 90 — rain almost horizontal, correct. Sine fails completely because the ratio exceeds one.
Tangent passes every test. Tangent is the right function.
